attributes.stem: A 63-year-old woman presents with a four month history of painless red lesions gradually spreading over her left arm (Image). Her arm has been swollen since a left total (radical) mastectomy followed by radiotherapy 20 years ago.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with vascular-appearing nodules with superficial bleeding of an oedematous appearing limb on a background of left total mastectomy and radiation 20 years prior. These clinical features paired with this history should raise suspicion for lymphangiosarcoma (also known as Stewart-Treves syndrome) as the diagnosis.

A radical (or total) mastectomy involves the complete removal of the breast, skin, pectoralis major and minor as well as the axillary contents including first, second, and third-order lymph nodes. This operation was originally used to enhance survival in breast cancer when compared to lumpectomies. Prior to the implementation of systemic therapies, radical mastectomy was the standard of care for treating breast cancer. Fortunately, due to improved surgical procedures and systemic therapies few women are now put through this mutilating operation, where chronic lymphoedema often follows the complete axillary lymph node clearance. Lymphangiosarcoma is a rare complication of long-standing lymphoedema. The tumor originates in vascular endothelial cells of the affected arm with lymphedema, not the lymphatic vessels. Sadly, lymphangiosarcoma has a poor prognosis with a 5-year survival of <5% with multimodality treatment.

As a side note, postmastectomy radiotherapy is an important adjuvant therapy for the locoregional management of some patients with breast cancer; it has been shown to reduce the rates of local recurrence and decrease breast cancer mortality.

Other options:

  • Recurrent breast cancer: Breast cancer represents a family of malignancies that can recur or relapse at any stage, the majority of breast cancer recurrences occur within the first five years of diagnosis but some may present after 20 years. Nevertheless, the clinical features (i.e., appearance) and significant history, in this case, suggest another diagnosis.

  • Neurofibromatosis: Neurofibromas are typical of NF-1 and are characterized as soft, painless nodules that typically manifest under or on the skin. They typically appear during adolescence with other features of NF-1 which may include seizures, café au lait spots, Lisch nodules, axillary freckling, and skeletal abnormalities (e.g., scoliosis). Given the stem above and the lack of NF-1 features, this diagnosis is unlikely.

  • Malignant melanoma: Are sometimes described as pigmented, pruritic, or bleeding skin lesions. Lesions are typically characterised by the ABCDE (+FG) acronym. Both the classic description and risk factors (i.e., UV exposure) for a malignant melanoma are missing from the stem and instead, the woman presents with vascular-appearing nodules with superficial bleeding on a background of left total mastectomy which makes a melanoma unlikely.

  • Radiation dermatitis: This is a common side effect of radiotherapy; affecting up to 95% of patients. The diagnosis of acute radiation dermatitis is clinical, based upon the dermatological findings paired with a history of radiotherapy. In comparison, chronic radiation dermatitis is characterized by dermal fibrosis with skin changes, like hypo- and hyper-pigmentation and atrophy which are absent seen here. This patient may have chronic radiation dermatitis of her breast, however, given the nodular and haemorrhagic appearance with a persistently oedematous limb, another diagnosis is more likely.
learningPoints: Lymphangiosarcoma is a malignant lesion that can occur in patients with chronic lymphedema. It has classically been due to chronic lymphoedema secondary to radical mastectomy.
references: Kwong, A., Sabel, M. Mastectomy [Internet] Uptodate.com 2021 [cited 04 Sep 2021]. Available from: <https://www.uptodate.com/contents/mastectomy>

Zhao, X., Tang, Y., Wang, S. et al. Locoregional recurrence patterns in women with breast cancer who have not undergone post-mastectomy radiotherapy. Radiat Oncol 15, 212 (2020). https://doi.org/10.1186/s13014-020-01637-w

Sepah YJ, Umer M, Qureshi A, Khan S. Lymphangiosarcoma of the arm presenting with lymphedema in a woman 16 years after mastectomy: a case report. Cases J. 2009;2:6887. Published 2009 Sep 1. doi:10.4076/1757-1626-2-6887

Chiugh, R., Sabel, M., Feng, F. Breast sarcoma: Epidemiology, risk factors, clinical presentation, diagnosis, and staging [Internet] Uptodate.com 2021 [cited 04 Sep 2021]. Available from: <https://www.uptodate.com/contents/breast-sarcoma-epidemiology-risk-factors-clinical-presentation-diagnosis-and-staging>
Wolf, J., Hong, A., iugh, R., Sabel, M., Feng, F. Radiation dermatitis [Internet] Uptodate.com 2021 [cited 04 Sep 2021]. Available from: < https://www.uptodate.com/contents/radiation-dermatitis>
